evidence: CSWP 39 §3 cites ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as one of the three mandatory-to-implement post-quantum algorithms for systems transitioning away from classical public-key cryptography.
CSWP 39 §3 cites ML-DSA (FIPS 204) as one of the three mandatory-to-implement post-quantum algorithms alongside ML-KEM and SLH-DSA.
CSWP 39 §3 cites SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) as the stateless hash-based signature algorithm required for cryptographic agility and backup-algorithm resilience.

CSWP 39 §4 applies the SP 800-131A Rev 3 transition framework as the normative deprecation schedule for classical algorithm migration; CSWP 39 guidance is a policy application of that broader transition framework.
CSWP 39 §2 references IR 8413 (Third Round Status Report) as the baseline selection rationale for the three standardized PQC algorithms; IR 8413 documents the selection decisions CSWP 39 builds upon.

CSWP 39 §5 references TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) as the primary network protocol context for PQC migration; hybrid key exchange via ML-KEM is the main deployment path for CSWP 39 recommendations in transport security.

CSWP 39 §5 references KEM integration in CMS (RFC 9629) as the messaging and certificate infrastructure path for PQC migration; organizations implementing CSWP 39 must address CMS-based key transport as well as TLS.
CNSA 2.0 §2.1 mandates ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as the required KEM algorithm for NSS; CNSA 2.0 is an implementation mandate that is a subset of the broader FIPS 203 standard.
CNSA 2.0 §2.2 mandates ML-DSA (FIPS 204) as the required digital signature algorithm for NSS; CNSA 2.0 is an implementation mandate that is a subset of the broader FIPS 204 standard.
CNSA 2.0 §2.2 mandates SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) as an approved stateless hash-based alternative signature algorithm for NSS; CNSA 2.0 guidance is a subset of the broader FIPS 205 standard.

SP 800-131A Rev 3 provides the normative algorithm deprecation and key-length schedule that CSWP 39 applies as a migration policy; SP 800-131A is the broader transition framework of which CSWP 39 guidance is a policy application.

ASD ISM (December 2024) §6 references the NIST PQC standardization programme and CSWP 39 cryptographic agility guidance as the foundation for ASD algorithm migration recommendations; Five Eyes alignment is explicit.
ASD ISM (December 2024) §6.2 lists ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as an approved post-quantum key encapsulation algorithm for Australian government systems.
NCSC Preparing for Quantum-Safe Cryptography references NIST PQC standards and cryptographic agility principles consistent with CSWP 39; Five Eyes coordination is referenced throughout.
NCSC Preparing for Quantum-Safe Cryptography endorses ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as the primary post-quantum KEM algorithm recommended for UK organisations transitioning away from RSA/ECC.
Canada TBS SPIN 2025-01 §3 references NIST cryptographic agility guidance (CSWP 39) as a key input to the GC PQC migration roadmap; Canadian policy mirrors NIST migration framework.
Canada TBS SPIN 2025-01 §4 endorses ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as an approved algorithm for Government of Canada systems transitioning to post-quantum cryptography.
NIST published ML-KEM (FIPS 203) in August 2024 as one of three inaugural post-quantum cryptography standards; this event marks the formal standardization milestone for the algorithm.
NIST published ML-DSA (FIPS 204) in August 2024 as one of three inaugural post-quantum cryptography standards; this event marks the formal standardization milestone for the algorithm.
NIST published SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) in August 2024 as one of three inaugural post-quantum cryptography standards; this event marks the formal standardization milestone for the algorithm.
NSA CNSA 2.0 mandates that new NSS acquisitions must include PQC algorithm support; the NSS Acquisitions deadline is a direct enforcement timeline for CNSA 2.0 compliance.

CSWP 39 was published concurrently with FIPS 203/204/205 as the migration guidance document; the standards finalization event is the prerequisite that enabled CSWP 39 to reference specific algorithm identifiers.
"PKCS#11 v3.2 adds post-quantum cryptography mechanisms: ML-KEM ... ML-DSA ... and SLH-DSA"

IR 8477 §3 (Feb 2024) uses SSDF (NIST SP 800-218) implementation examples as one of its primary worked examples for documenting concept relationships, demonstrating how an implementation-recommendation framework maps onto IR 8477's methodology.

NIS2 Directive Art. 21(2)(h) mandates state-of-the-art cryptography for essential entities. ENISA 2024 PQC Technical Guideline §4.2 lists ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as the recommended post-quantum KEM for NIS2 compliance.
ENISA 2024 PQC Technical Guideline §4.3 lists ML-DSA (FIPS 204) as the recommended post-quantum digital signature algorithm for NIS2-covered entities implementing crypto-agility.
ENISA 2024 PQC Technical Guideline §4.4 lists SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) as an alternative stateless hash-based signature for NIS2 compliance, recommended for high-assurance use cases.

DORA RTS on ICT risk management (Delegated Regulation EU 2024/1774) Art. 14 requires financial entities to use up-to-date encryption. ENISA Financial Threat Landscape 2024 recommends ML-KEM (FIPS 203) for DORA crypto-agility implementation.

DORA Recital 45 requires financial entities to adopt structured cryptographic migration plans. ENISA DORA PQC guidance (2024) cites NIST CSWP 39 as the reference framework for quantum-safe transition planning.

ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A A.8.24 mandates use of cryptographic controls and requires organisations to follow current standards. ISO/IEC 27002:2022 §8.24 guidance directs implementers to post-quantum algorithms including ML-KEM (FIPS 203).
ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A A.8.24 cryptographic controls requirement. ISO/IEC 27002:2022 §8.24 references NIST PQC standards including ML-DSA (FIPS 204) for digital signature agility.

PCI DSS v4.0 Requirement 4.2.1 mandates strong cryptography for data in transit. PCI SSC FAQ on Quantum Computing Threats (2024) explicitly references NIST CSWP 39 as the authoritative guidance for PCI-compliant quantum-safe algorithm selection.

PCI DSS v4.0 Requirement 3.5 requires cryptographic key management to anticipate algorithm deprecation. PCI SSC recommends ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as the target key encapsulation mechanism for PCI-DSS environments transitioning from RSA/ECC.
BSI TR-02102-1 Version 2025 §6 explicitly lists ML-KEM (FIPS 203 / CRYSTALS-Kyber) as a Category 1 recommended post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism. BSI recommends deployment by 2026.
BSI TR-02102-1 Version 2025 §7 explicitly lists ML-DSA (FIPS 204 / CRYSTALS-Dilithium) as a Category 1 recommended post-quantum digital signature. BSI recommends migration from ECDSA/RSA by 2026.
BSI TR-02102-1 Version 2025 §7 lists SLH-DSA (FIPS 205 / SPHINCS+) as a recommended stateless hash-based signature alternative for high-assurance deployments where ML-DSA is not applicable.

SWIFT Customer Security Controls Framework v2025 Control 2.9 mandates use of strong encryption and cryptographic agility. SWIFT CSP implementation guide references FIPS 203 (ML-KEM) as the target KEM for SWIFT financial messaging infrastructure.
G7 Cyber Expert Group PQC report (October 2024) §3.2 explicitly references NIST CSWP 39 as the primary reference framework for financial sector quantum-safe migration roadmaps.
G7 CEG PQC report (2024) §4.1 recommends that G7 financial institutions adopt ML-KEM (FIPS 203) for key encapsulation in payment and messaging infrastructure, with a target adoption date of 2030.

IEC 62443-3-3 SR 4.3 requires industrial control systems to use FIPS-validated cryptographic modules. IEC 62443 Technical Committee alignment document (2024) references FIPS 203 as the designated KEM for OT/ICS quantum-safe key establishment.

NERC CIP-005-7 and CIP-007-6 require strong encryption for bulk electric system communications. NERC CEATI quantum readiness guidance (2024) references NIST CSWP 39 for critical infrastructure quantum-safe planning.

EU Recommendation 2024/1101 §3(c) instructs Member States to develop post-quantum migration roadmaps following established international frameworks. ENISA implementation note cites NIST CSWP 39 as the reference methodology.

EU Recommendation 2024/1101 §4 calls for adoption of quantum-resistant algorithms standardised by recognised bodies. ENISA guidance explicitly endorses ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as a conformant algorithm for EU public sector systems.

ETSI TS 103 744 specifies CRYSTALS-Kyber (ML-KEM) as a post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism for ETSI security standards. FIPS 203 standardises the same algorithm (ML-KEM); the specifications are technically equivalent.
ETSI TS 103 744 specifies CRYSTALS-Dilithium (ML-DSA) as a post-quantum digital signature. FIPS 204 standardises the same algorithm (ML-DSA); the specifications are technically equivalent.
CRYPTREC 2024 Recommended Cryptographic Algorithms list includes ML-KEM (CRYSTALS-Kyber) aligned with FIPS 203 for post-quantum key encapsulation in Japanese government systems.
CRYPTREC 2024 list includes ML-DSA (CRYSTALS-Dilithium) aligned with FIPS 204 for post-quantum digital signatures in Japanese government and critical infrastructure.

MAS Circular on Cyber Resilience (2024) §6.2 requires Singapore financial institutions to develop quantum-safe cryptography transition plans. MAS references NIST CSWP 39 as the international benchmark for PQC migration planning.

NSA CNSA 2.0 (CNSSP-15 2022) mandates specific PQC algorithms (ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, LMS/XMSS) for NSS systems by 2033. CNSA 2.0 is a subset implementation of NIST CSWP 39 recommendations, restricted to NSS/NatSec use cases.
UK NCSC PQC Migration Guidance (2024) §4 explicitly recommends ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as the post-quantum KEM for UK government and CNI systems transitioning from RSA/DH/ECDH.
UK NCSC PQC Migration Guidance (2024) §5 explicitly recommends ML-DSA (FIPS 204) as the post-quantum digital signature standard for UK government and critical national infrastructure.

ASD ISM 2024 Cryptographic Guidance ISM-1917 mandates post-quantum key encapsulation for new deployments. ASD ISM ISM-1920 explicitly references FIPS 203 (ML-KEM) as the approved algorithm for Australian government systems.
